title: Creating Websites for Research Outreach
text:
Quarto allows you to generate full websites which can then be deployed for free using services such as GitHub Pages or Netlify. This provides an easy solution to create web content for research groups, project pages, course and teaching materials, as well as personal academic profiles.
In the exercise for this section, you will create a simple website to share your research outputs.


Doing some form of outreach activities has become increasingly important for:

Increasing your professional visibility
Communicating research outputs to audiences beyond academic journals
Sharing research materials and methods with the broader community







The websites functionality of Quarto is built on top of an existing framework called Bootstrap.
Like all websites Quarto website pages render as .HTML files. In many IDEs such as Rstudio, VScode and Positron these can be viewed locally directly in the ‘viewer pane’ by clicking the ‘Preview’ button at the top of the file editor window ir right clicking on the file itself.


Like all Quarto projects, websites should use a project-level _quarto.yml file to define the website structure and formatting. This allows you to easily manage navigation, themes, and other shared settings across all pages of the site.




Websites must have an index.qmd file in the root directory which serves as the front page of the site. This page can include any content you like, but it is often used to provide an overview of the site and highlight key information.





The same Markdown, YAML, callouts, and code chunk patterns appear in websites too. They can also include HTML blocks.






Tip



Websites can include other Quarto ocntent such as dashboards and RevealJS presentations as part of the site, but they are not required to be built with Quarto. You can also include any other HTML content you like.






Here are some real websites that we have built with Quarto:

Personal academic profile
NASCENT-Peru — a multi-lingual research project website (discussed further in the advanced topics section)
Academic group website
LCSfFE Handbook — web version of an instructional guide for building field monitoring systems

The website you are reading right now is also built with Quarto.
Quarto isn’t limited to academic use either — it works just as well for personal projects:

Wedding website

Go to the websites exercise

title: Writing Manuscripts
text:
Quarto can support manuscript writing by keeping narrative text, citations, figures, tables, and optional code in a single reproducible workflow. It is especially helpful when the same article needs to move between collaborator-friendly and submission-ready formats.


Manuscripts often require repeated changes across many components:

main text
references
tables and figures
supplementary material
multiple export formats

Quarto reduces friction by keeping these connected rather than scattered across separate systems.





The same source can often render to HTML, PDF, and DOCX, which is useful for review, sharing, and submission preparation.



Quarto works with bibliographies and citation styles, allowing references to stay embedded in the writing workflow.



When analysis is embedded, manuscript results become easier to update and verify.




A research team drafting a journal article might use HTML for internal review, DOCX for collaborators who prefer tracked changes, and PDF for a polished reading version. The content stays aligned because it comes from the same source file.






TipPractical starting point



You do not need to begin with a fully automated manuscript. Even using Quarto first as a well-structured writing environment already creates value.


Go to the manuscripts exercise

title: Interactive Dashboards
text:
Quarto can also support interactive outputs, including dashboard-style communication. For researchers, this can be a useful bridge between formal publication and exploratory sharing with collaborators or stakeholders.


Some audiences need more than a static paper or slide deck. They may benefit from:

filterable summaries
updated visuals
browsable indicators
lightweight interaction around results

Quarto dashboards can serve these needs while staying close to the same publishing ecosystem.





Dashboards can organize findings into cards, sections, and panels that are easier to scan than a long narrative report.



Interactive views can still draw on the same documented analysis pipeline used elsewhere in a project.



Researchers do not need to begin with a complex application. Even a small dashboard with a few indicators can be valuable.




A project team monitoring restoration sites could create a dashboard summarizing site status, recent measurements, and key indicators for partners who do not need the full technical report.






WarningScope reminder



Dashboards can become technically demanding quickly. For a beginner workshop, it is often better to teach the concept and a simple starter pattern rather than a full production system.


Go to the dashboards exercise

title: 1 AI assistance
text:
1 AI assistance
Add the quarto-authoring skill for IDE agents from: https://mcpmarket.com/tools/skills/quarto-authoring-migration
$quarto-authoring I want to develop a website with guided excercises to explore the many uses of Quarto for academic researchers. It should have sections in seperate qmd files covering the following topics: - Short intro to Quarto: Whjat it is, who made it, relation Rmarkdown, key strengths: I.e. universal syntax, multiple output formats from single files simultanesously, embedded executable code chunks in multiple languages. - Intro to markdown syntax for beginners, with a focus on Quarto. - Intro to YAML headers - Installing Quarto
Seperate sections for each ‘use-case’ for academics, each section should have an explanatory introduction and an accompanying guided exercise: - Documenting research in progress (i.e a lab notebook) - Writing Manuscripts - Creating presentations - Creating project and personal websites for outreach and professional visibility - Interactive dashboards
Advanced topics: - templates and extensions - brand.yml
Here is a text I produced to describe the workshop: Title: One Tool to Rule Them All: Quarto for Documentation, Presentations, Manuscripts and Outreach
This hands-on workshop will explore Quarto, an open-source scientific and technical publishing system from Posit (makers of RStudio), as a unified solution for academic communication. A key strength of Quarto is its ability to generate multiple output formats (e.g., PDF, HTML, LaTeX, and DOCX) from a single source document, while supporting executable code in multiple programming languages (Python, R, Julia, and Observable). This enables a more efficient, integrated, and reproducible workflow across all stages of research and dissemination. Through a combination of short presentations and guided exercises, participants will learn how Quarto can be used to produce the full spectrum of academic outputs: documentation of exploratory research and experiments; submission-ready, reproducible journal articles; conference presentations; project and personal websites for outreach and professional visibility; as well as interactive dashboards and personalized mass communication. The workshop is particularly well suited for early career researchers who are still developing their workflows, as well as more experienced researchers seeking a more integrated approach to managing research outputs. All workshop materials will be provided as an open-access website for future reference. Basic familiarity with at least one programming language (R, Python, or Julia) is helpful but not required. By the end of the session, participants will be equipped to streamline their workflows and produce professional, reproducible outputs across multiple formats from a single source.

text:
4 Writing a Book or Open Textbook
Quarto’s book format makes it straightforward to author and publish multi-chapter documents online. Utrecht University has put together a practical guide on creating open textbooks with Quarto and GitHub Pages:

Open Textbooks with Quarto — Utrecht University

title: Go Further
text:
5 The Quarto Ecosystem
Awesome Quarto is a community-maintained list of Quarto-related talks, tools, templates, examples, and articles — a great place to discover what others are building and find inspiration for your own projects.

title: What Is Quarto?
text:
Quarto is an open-source publishing system developed by Posit. It is built for technical and scientific communication, which makes it especially useful for research settings where writing, analysis, and presentation often belong together.
Quarto is closely related to R Markdown, and many researchers meet it as the newer, broader continuation of ideas that became popular in the R Markdown ecosystem. The biggest shift is that Quarto was designed from the start as a multi-language, multi-format publishing system rather than as a tool mainly associated with R.


Research outputs rarely stay in one format. The same project may need:

notes during exploration
a manuscript draft
conference slides
a project website
an interactive communication piece

Quarto supports all of these with a common syntax and shared project structure.





Quarto uses readable plain text files with the .qmd extension. That makes documents easier to version, review, and reuse than proprietary binary formats.



A single source file can often render to several outputs such as HTML, PDF, and DOCX. This reduces duplication and keeps content aligned across formats.



Quarto can include executable code chunks in languages such as R, Python, Julia, and Observable JavaScript. That supports reproducible analysis and reporting without forcing everyone into the same language.




A doctoral researcher might use:

one Quarto file as a lab notebook for ongoing observations
another as a manuscript draft that renders to PDF and DOCX
a slide deck generated from the same project for a conference talk
a simple website to share outputs and project updates

That is the larger theme of this workshop: one authoring system, many academic uses.






ImportantQuarto, Markdown, and YAML



Most Quarto files combine three ingredients:

YAML for metadata and configuration
Markdown for the written content
optional code chunks for computation and generated output






Continue to Markdown for Beginners to learn the authoring syntax that most Quarto pages rely on.

title: Installing Quarto
text: Before creating projects, participants need Quarto itself installed on their computer. They also need at least one way to edit plain text files, and optionally a programming environment if they want to run code chunks.

title: Installing Quarto
text:
2 Why This Matters For Researchers
A clear installation path lowers the barrier to entry. Many workshop participants are interested in the publishing workflow first and only later decide how much computation they want to embed.

title: Installing Quarto
text:
3 What To Install

3.1 Quarto CLI
The Quarto command line interface is the core tool. It handles rendering and project management.


3.2 An Editor
Participants can use:

Positron or RStudio
VS Code
another plain text editor they are comfortable with



3.3 Optional Language Runtime
If they want executable code chunks, they will also need at least one language runtime such as:

R
Python
Julia

title: Installing Quarto
text:
4 Suggested Installation Workflow

Download Quarto from the official website.
Install it using the standard installer for your operating system.
Open a terminal and run quarto check.
Confirm your editor recognizes .qmd files.
If you want live code execution, confirm that R, Python, or Julia is also available.

title: Installing Quarto
text:
5 Concrete Academic Example
A participant who mainly wants to create websites and notes may only need Quarto and an editor at first. A participant building reproducible reports with figures from analysis will also want a runtime such as R or Python installed.






TipWorkshop-friendly setup



For a mixed audience, it helps to frame code execution as optional. Participants can still learn a lot from Quarto even before they start running embedded code.

title: Installing Quarto
text:
6 Self-Check
After installation, participants should be able to answer yes to these questions:

Can I open or create a .qmd file?
Can I run quarto check in a terminal?
Do I know which editor I will use during the workshop?

title: Installing Quarto
text:
7 Next Step
Once the foundations are in place, move into the first use case: Documenting Research in Progress.

title: Exercise: Build a Short Slide Deck
text:
Create a minimal Quarto presentation that could support a short research talk.


You should know:

basic Markdown headings
how YAML configures document format
that RevealJS is one common Quarto presentation format




You have been asked to give a five-minute update on a project about drought impacts on vegetation. You need a quick slide deck with a title slide, research question, approach, and next steps.




Create a file called talk.qmd.
Add a YAML header with a title and format: revealjs.
Create a first slide with the title and presenter name.
Add slides for research question, methods, key finding, and next steps.
Keep each slide concise using bullets instead of paragraphs.
Add one placeholder line for a future figure.




---
title: "Drought Impacts on Vegetation"
format: revealjs
---

## Research Question

- How did vegetation response differ across sites?

## Methods

- Remote sensing summary
- Field validation



Add speaker notes or a final slide with contact information.



Your slide deck should:

have a clear sequence
be appropriate for a short academic talk
show how Quarto can switch from document writing to presentation authoring




Ask yourself:

Would each slide be readable in a live talk?
Is there one main message per slide?
Can this deck grow into a fuller presentation later?

title: Exercise: Build a Lab Notebook Page
text:
Create a Quarto document for documenting ongoing research activity in a structured, readable way.


You should be familiar with:

basic Markdown headings and lists
the idea of a YAML header
opening or creating a .qmd file




You are working on a small restoration ecology project. You want a notebook entry that captures this week’s work, next steps, and one optional code area for later use.




Create a new file called notebook.qmd.
Add a YAML header with a title, your name, today’s date, and format: html.
Add a heading for the current week.
Write a short paragraph describing what you did.
Add a bullet list of observations or decisions.
Add a numbered list of next steps.
Add a fenced code block or commented placeholder showing where a future chunk could go.




---
title: "Restoration Project Notebook"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format: html
---

## Week 1

This week I reviewed the sampling plan and checked the latest field observations.

- Confirmed plot IDs
- Flagged missing measurements
- Noted one sensor calibration issue

1. Recheck the field sheet
2. Contact collaborators about the missing values
3. Prepare the next visit



Add a callout block with a reminder about one issue to revisit next week.



By the end, you should have a simple notebook page that:

renders as a readable HTML document
separates narrative, decisions, and tasks clearly
could be reused as a template for future entries




Ask yourself:

Does the page make sense if I read it again in a month?
Can another team member understand what happened this week?
Is the structure simple enough to reuse consistently?

title: 1 Design System: One Tool to Rule Them All — Quarto Workshop
text:
Creative North Star: “The Quartile Press”
This is an editorial press rendered in HTML. The page is warm paper stock; the type is set, not typed; and the system carries four signature accent colors — indigo, coral, moss, amber — as its four “quarters,” one per family of Quarto output. The whole site is its own proof of concept: a Quarto site that teaches Quarto, so every surface has to read as something a careful researcher would be proud to publish. Restraint is the house style. Warmth comes from the paper, the Newsreader serif, and the quartile accents, never from effects.
The system rejects four things by name. It is not the out-of-the-box Bootstrap/Quarto default theme — undifferentiated, obviously templated. It is not a corporate SaaS landing page — no gradient hero, no hero-metric stat blocks, no identical feature-card grids, no buzzword copy. It is not overdesigned — no decorative animation, no glassmorphism, nothing that competes with reading. And it is done with AI-slop card scaffolding: numbered section markers (01 · / 02 ·) and colored left-stripe borders are recognised tells, slated for removal, never to be extended.
The reader is either mid-workshop or returning in a hurry; both must find the right page fast. On interior reference pages, structure and findability outrank decoration. On the landing page, the design is allowed to carry the message.
Key Characteristics: - Warm paper ground (#FBF7F0), deep indigo-black ink (#1A1B2E) — never pure white, never pure black. - Four-accent “quartile” system, used sparingly and meaningfully, one accent per use-case family. - Serif display (Newsreader) over sans body (IBM Plex Sans) over mono (IBM Plex Mono) — a three-axis contrast, not three competing voices. - Soft ambient depth at rest, a gentle 2px lift on hover. Nothing floats. - First-party light and dark brand.yml themes, both contrast-checked to WCAG 2.1 AA.



A warm-neutral ground carrying four deliberately spaced accents — one cool-deep, one warm, one green, one gold — so each Quarto use-case can own a hue without the palette fragmenting.



Deep Indigo (#3D2F8C): The brand spine. Links, the active sidebar item, primary emphasis, the Foundations quarter. Conveys depth and trust. In dark theme it lifts to #A899FF for contrast.





Warm Coral (#D9543F): Energy and warning. Callout accents (important/caution), the italic emphasis inside the page title, the Notebooks quarter, text selection. The single warmest note in the system; deploy it where you want the eye to land.





Forest Moss (#2F7D6A): Code and success. Inline code text on a moss-soft fill, success callouts, the Manuscripts quarter. The “this works” color.
Golden Amber (#C98A2B): Signal and highlight. Warning callouts, the Outreach/Advanced quarters. A measured gold, never neon.





Paper (#FBF7F0): The page ground. Warm off-white, the default body background.
Paper 2 (#F4EFE4): Subtle raised surfaces — cards, code blocks, callout bodies.
Paper 3 (#E8E1D2): Borders and dividers at full strength.
Ink (#1A1B2E): Primary text and the primary button fill. A deep indigo-black, not neutral black.
Ink 2 (#3A3B52): Secondary text, lead paragraphs, nav links at rest.
Muted (#6B6275): Tertiary text, captions, section numbers, label/eyebrow text. Audit: muted on paper is the system’s tightest contrast pairing — verify ≥4.5:1 before using it for anything a reader must actually read; reserve it for genuinely secondary text.





Indigo / Coral / Moss / Amber Soft (#EDE9FA / #FBE7E1 / #E0EFE9 / #F5EAD3): Fills for callout bodies, inline code, blockquotes, and chips. Each is a near-white wash of its parent accent.




The Quartile Rule. Each of the four accents maps to a use-case family and keeps that meaning everywhere: indigo = Foundations/links, coral = energy/notebooks, moss = code/success, amber = signal/advanced. Never pick an accent for decoration alone; pick it because it carries that meaning here.
The Warm-Ground Rule. Pure #FFFFFF and pure #000000 are forbidden. The page is paper and ink. Every neutral carries a trace of the brand’s warmth.




Display Font: Newsreader (with Iowan Old Style, Georgia, serif) Body Font: IBM Plex Sans (with system-ui, -apple-system, sans-serif) Label/Mono Font: IBM Plex Mono (with ui-monospace, Menlo, monospace)
Character: A literary serif paired with a clear, slightly technical humanist sans and an honest mono. The pairing works on a true contrast axis (serif display + sans body + mono labels), so it reads as edited, not indecisive. Newsreader supplies the editorial warmth; Plex Sans keeps long instructional prose comfortable; Plex Mono signals “this is code or a literal token.”



Display (Newsreader 600, clamp(2.2rem, 4.2vw, 3.4rem), line-height 1.04, tracking -0.012em): Page titles only. Italic <em> inside a title renders in Warm Coral.
Headline (Newsreader 600, 1.9rem, line-height 1.15): h2 section headings.
Title (Newsreader 600, 1.45rem, line-height 1.2): h3 subsection headings.
Body (IBM Plex Sans 400, 1.0625rem, line-height 1.65): All prose. Cap measure at 65–75ch.
Subtitle (Newsreader 400 italic, clamp(1.1rem, 1.6vw, 1.35rem), Ink 2): The line under a page title.
Label (IBM Plex Mono 500, 0.74em, tracking 0.04em, UPPERCASE): Chips, tags, table headers, the employer line on team cards, sidebar/TOC section labels. ≤4 words only.
Mono (IBM Plex Mono 400, 0.92em, line-height 1.55): Code blocks and inline code.




The Three-Voice Rule. Three families, no more: serif to display, sans to read, mono to signal code. Adding a fourth typeface is forbidden; hierarchy comes from weight and scale within these three.
The Caps-Label-Only Rule. Uppercase is permitted only for labels of four words or fewer (chips, table headers, the mono section labels). Sentences and headings are never set in all caps.




The system is mostly flat, lifted by a single soft ambient shadow that warms rather than darkens (it is mixed from the ink color, not black). Surfaces sit quietly on the paper at rest and rise a gentle 2px on hover. Depth is a response to interaction, not a permanent decoration.



Ambient rest (box-shadow: 0 1px 2px color-mix(in oklab, #1A1B2E 4%, transparent), 0 6px 24px -10px color-mix(in oklab, #1A1B2E 10%, transparent)): The resting shadow under cards, callouts, and code blocks. Barely there; it separates the surface from the paper without announcing itself.
Lifted hover (box-shadow: 0 4px 20px rgba(26,27,46,0.12), 0 2px 6px rgba(26,27,46,0.07) paired with transform: translateY(-2px)): The interactive state for clickable cards.




The Warm-Shadow Rule. Shadows are mixed from Ink (#1A1B2E), never pure black. A neutral-black shadow on warm paper reads as cheap and cold.
The Lift-On-Intent Rule. The 2px rise is reserved for surfaces the reader can actually click. Don’t lift static containers; the motion is a signal, not a flourish.







Shape: Fully pill (999px radius), weight 500, padding 0.55rem 1.1rem.
Primary: Ink fill (#1A1B2E), Paper text. On hover the fill shifts to Deep Indigo — the button “warms into” the brand color rather than just darkening.
Outline: Transparent fill, Ink text, hairline rule border. On hover, a Paper 2 fill and an Ink 2 border.
Hover / Focus: Color transitions ~120ms ease. Keep a visible :focus-visible ring for keyboard users.





Style: Mono Label type (uppercase, tracked), pill shape, a soft-accent fill with its parent accent as text (e.g. indigo-soft bg / indigo text).
Use: Exercise labels, metadata badges. Never as decorative sprinkles.





Corner Style: 0.75rem (lg) radius.
Background: Paper 2 on the Paper ground.
Shadow Strategy: Ambient rest → Lifted hover (see Elevation). Clickable cards get cursor: pointer and the 2px lift.
Border: A single hairline rule on all four sides — 1px solid of the 12%-ink rule color. No colored left-stripe. (The current homepage subject cards and team cards still carry a 3px colored border-left; that is the flagged slop and is being removed, not the pattern to copy.)
Internal Padding: ~1.1rem to 1.2rem.





Style: Hairline rule border, Paper or Paper 2 fill, md (0.5rem) radius, body type.
Focus: Border shifts to Deep Indigo with a soft indigo focus ring. No glow, no glassmorphism.
Placeholder: Must clear 4.5:1 — use Ink 2, not Muted.





Navbar: Paper background with a hairline bottom rule and a faint saturate(1.1) blur(6px) backdrop. Brand in Newsreader; nav links in Plex Sans 500, Ink 2 at rest → Ink on hover/active.
Sidebar: Mono uppercase section labels (Muted); item text in Ink 2 → Deep Indigo on hover/active. The active item carries a 2px indigo left-accent — the one sanctioned left-accent in the system, because it marks position in a list rather than decorating a card.
TOC (right rail): Small (0.88rem), mono uppercase title, active entry in Deep Indigo.





Style: Paper 2 body, lg radius, hairline rule, ambient-rest shadow, header in Plex Sans 600. The colored accent rides the left edge (4px) and the header text matches: indigo (note), moss (tip), amber (warning), coral (important/caution). This left-accent is Quarto’s native callout idiom and is the one place a thick colored left-edge is allowed — it is not licence to put stripes on cards.





Inline: Moss text on Moss Soft fill, sm radius, a hairline moss-tinted border, 0.9em.
Block: Paper 2 fill, hairline rule, md radius, 0.92em Plex Mono at line-height 1.55. Copy button in Muted → Deep Indigo on hover.





Indigo Soft fill, 3px indigo left border, Newsreader italic at 1.08em. The editorial pull-quote voice of the system.




Horizontal rules render not as a line but as a 10px four-square mark: indigo / coral / moss / amber in a 2×2, the system’s logo in miniature. Use it as a section break with meaning; it is the press’s colophon.







Do keep the warm paper-and-ink ground. Background is Paper (#FBF7F0), text is Ink (#1A1B2E).
Do honor the Quartile Rule — each accent carries its use-case meaning everywhere it appears.
Do border cards on all four sides with a single hairline rule; let the ambient shadow and the 2px hover lift carry separation and interactivity.
Do keep prose at 65–75ch and body type at line-height 1.65.
Do restrict uppercase to mono labels of ≤4 words.
Do mix shadows from Ink, never black, and reserve the 2px lift for clickable surfaces.
Do provide a prefers-reduced-motion alternative for every transition, and a visible :focus-visible ring everywhere.
Do verify Muted-on-Paper hits ≥4.5:1 before using it for readable text; bump to Ink 2 if it’s close.





Don’t ship the generic default docs theme look — the out-of-the-box Bootstrap/Quarto template is an explicit anti-reference.
Don’t build a corporate SaaS landing — no gradient hero, no hero-metric stat blocks, no identical feature-card grids, no buzzword copy (“streamline”, “empower”, “seamless”, “world-class”).
Don’t overdesign — no decorative animation, no glassmorphism, no effect that competes with reading.
Don’t use colored left-stripe borders greater than 1px on cards, list items, or grid items (the homepage subject/team cards currently do; they are being reworked). The callout left-accent and the active-sidebar accent are the only sanctioned exceptions.
Don’t scaffold sections with numbered markers (01 · / 02 ·) by reflex — numbers earn their place only in a genuine ordered sequence.
Don’t use gradient text (background-clip: text) or pure-white/pure-black anywhere.
Don’t add a fourth typeface, and don’t set sentences or headings in all caps.
Don’t use Muted gray for body text or placeholders — it fails contrast on warm paper.

title: 1 Design System: One Tool to Rule Them All — Quarto Workshop
text:
Creative North Star: “The Quartile Press”
This is an editorial press rendered in HTML. The page is warm paper stock; the type is set, not typed; and the system carries four signature accent colors — indigo, coral, moss, amber — as its four “quarters,” one per family of Quarto output. The whole site is its own proof of concept: a Quarto site that teaches Quarto, so every surface has to read as something a careful researcher would be proud to publish. Restraint is the house style. Warmth comes from the paper, the Newsreader serif, and the quartile accents, never from effects.
The system rejects four things by name. It is not the out-of-the-box Bootstrap/Quarto default theme — undifferentiated, obviously templated. It is not a corporate SaaS landing page — no gradient hero, no hero-metric stat blocks, no identical feature-card grids, no buzzword copy. It is not overdesigned — no decorative animation, no glassmorphism, nothing that competes with reading. And it is done with AI-slop card scaffolding: numbered section markers (01 · / 02 ·) and colored left-stripe borders are recognised tells, slated for removal, never to be extended.
The reader is either mid-workshop or returning in a hurry; both must find the right page fast. On interior reference pages, structure and findability outrank decoration. On the landing page, the design is allowed to carry the message.
Key Characteristics: - Warm paper ground (#FBF7F0), deep indigo-black ink (#1A1B2E) — never pure white, never pure black. - Four-accent “quartile” system, used sparingly and meaningfully, one accent per use-case family. - Serif display (Newsreader) over sans body (IBM Plex Sans) over mono (IBM Plex Mono) — a three-axis contrast, not three competing voices. - Soft ambient depth at rest, a gentle 2px lift on hover. Nothing floats. - First-party light and dark brand.yml themes, both contrast-checked to WCAG 2.1 AA.

title: 1 Design System: One Tool to Rule Them All — Quarto Workshop
text:
A warm-neutral ground carrying four deliberately spaced accents — one cool-deep, one warm, one green, one gold — so each Quarto use-case can own a hue without the palette fragmenting.



Deep Indigo (#3D2F8C): The brand spine. Links, the active sidebar item, primary emphasis, the Foundations quarter. Conveys depth and trust. In dark theme it lifts to #A899FF for contrast.





Warm Coral (#D9543F): Energy and warning. Callout accents (important/caution), the italic emphasis inside the page title, the Notebooks quarter, text selection. The single warmest note in the system; deploy it where you want the eye to land.





Forest Moss (#2F7D6A): Code and success. Inline code text on a moss-soft fill, success callouts, the Manuscripts quarter. The “this works” color.
Golden Amber (#C98A2B): Signal and highlight. Warning callouts, the Outreach/Advanced quarters. A measured gold, never neon.





Paper (#FBF7F0): The page ground. Warm off-white, the default body background.
Paper 2 (#F4EFE4): Subtle raised surfaces — cards, code blocks, callout bodies.
Paper 3 (#E8E1D2): Borders and dividers at full strength.
Ink (#1A1B2E): Primary text and the primary button fill. A deep indigo-black, not neutral black.
Ink 2 (#3A3B52): Secondary text, lead paragraphs, nav links at rest.
Muted (#6B6275): Tertiary text, captions, section numbers, label/eyebrow text. Audit: muted on paper is the system’s tightest contrast pairing — verify ≥4.5:1 before using it for anything a reader must actually read; reserve it for genuinely secondary text.





Indigo / Coral / Moss / Amber Soft (#EDE9FA / #FBE7E1 / #E0EFE9 / #F5EAD3): Fills for callout bodies, inline code, blockquotes, and chips. Each is a near-white wash of its parent accent.




The Quartile Rule. Each of the four accents maps to a use-case family and keeps that meaning everywhere: indigo = Foundations/links, coral = energy/notebooks, moss = code/success, amber = signal/advanced. Never pick an accent for decoration alone; pick it because it carries that meaning here.
The Warm-Ground Rule. Pure #FFFFFF and pure #000000 are forbidden. The page is paper and ink. Every neutral carries a trace of the brand’s warmth.

title: 1 Design System: One Tool to Rule Them All — Quarto Workshop
text:
Display Font: Newsreader (with Iowan Old Style, Georgia, serif) Body Font: IBM Plex Sans (with system-ui, -apple-system, sans-serif) Label/Mono Font: IBM Plex Mono (with ui-monospace, Menlo, monospace)
Character: A literary serif paired with a clear, slightly technical humanist sans and an honest mono. The pairing works on a true contrast axis (serif display + sans body + mono labels), so it reads as edited, not indecisive. Newsreader supplies the editorial warmth; Plex Sans keeps long instructional prose comfortable; Plex Mono signals “this is code or a literal token.”



Display (Newsreader 600, clamp(2.2rem, 4.2vw, 3.4rem), line-height 1.04, tracking -0.012em): Page titles only. Italic <em> inside a title renders in Warm Coral.
Headline (Newsreader 600, 1.9rem, line-height 1.15): h2 section headings.
Title (Newsreader 600, 1.45rem, line-height 1.2): h3 subsection headings.
Body (IBM Plex Sans 400, 1.0625rem, line-height 1.65): All prose. Cap measure at 65–75ch.
Subtitle (Newsreader 400 italic, clamp(1.1rem, 1.6vw, 1.35rem), Ink 2): The line under a page title.
Label (IBM Plex Mono 500, 0.74em, tracking 0.04em, UPPERCASE): Chips, tags, table headers, the employer line on team cards, sidebar/TOC section labels. ≤4 words only.
Mono (IBM Plex Mono 400, 0.92em, line-height 1.55): Code blocks and inline code.




The Three-Voice Rule. Three families, no more: serif to display, sans to read, mono to signal code. Adding a fourth typeface is forbidden; hierarchy comes from weight and scale within these three.
The Caps-Label-Only Rule. Uppercase is permitted only for labels of four words or fewer (chips, table headers, the mono section labels). Sentences and headings are never set in all caps.

title: 1 Design System: One Tool to Rule Them All — Quarto Workshop
text:
The system is mostly flat, lifted by a single soft ambient shadow that warms rather than darkens (it is mixed from the ink color, not black). Surfaces sit quietly on the paper at rest and rise a gentle 2px on hover. Depth is a response to interaction, not a permanent decoration.



Ambient rest (box-shadow: 0 1px 2px color-mix(in oklab, #1A1B2E 4%, transparent), 0 6px 24px -10px color-mix(in oklab, #1A1B2E 10%, transparent)): The resting shadow under cards, callouts, and code blocks. Barely there; it separates the surface from the paper without announcing itself.
Lifted hover (box-shadow: 0 4px 20px rgba(26,27,46,0.12), 0 2px 6px rgba(26,27,46,0.07) paired with transform: translateY(-2px)): The interactive state for clickable cards.




The Warm-Shadow Rule. Shadows are mixed from Ink (#1A1B2E), never pure black. A neutral-black shadow on warm paper reads as cheap and cold.
The Lift-On-Intent Rule. The 2px rise is reserved for surfaces the reader can actually click. Don’t lift static containers; the motion is a signal, not a flourish.

title: Using brand.yml
text:
brand.yml is a Quarto branding file used to define shared visual identity such as colors, logos, and typography across outputs. It is particularly useful for websites, presentations, and other materials that should feel consistent.


Research groups, projects, and centers often want their outputs to look connected. Instead of styling each document separately, brand.yml provides a shared source of branding choices.



Depending on the project, a branding file may define:

color palette
logo assets
typography choices
reusable visual identity settings




A collaborative project can use one brand configuration so that its website, slide deck, and outreach documents share a recognizable visual style.



In this workshop, brand.yml should be introduced as an advanced organizational tool rather than a first-day requirement. Participants should leave knowing:

what problem it solves
when it becomes useful
why consistent branding can improve professional visibility and project coherence







NoteKeep the priority straight



Branding matters, but structure and clarity matter first. A plain, well-organized Quarto project is more valuable than a beautifully branded one that is hard to maintain.





If this workshop later expands, brand.yml would pair nicely with a follow-up session on custom themes, templates, and project-wide style conventions.

title: Templates and Extensions
text:
Once participants are comfortable with Quarto basics, templates and extensions provide ways to speed up repeated work and add specialized functionality.


Academic workflows often repeat familiar patterns:

manuscript structures
recurring workshop materials
shared project websites
journal-specific output requirements

Templates reduce setup time, and extensions can add features that are not included by default.



Templates are useful when you want a consistent starting point for recurring work. A research group might maintain a preferred manuscript skeleton or workshop-page pattern so new documents begin with the right sections already in place.



Quarto extensions can add custom formats, filters, shortcodes, or presentation features. They are installed into a project and become part of its publishing toolkit.
Example install command:
quarto add quarto-ext/fontawesome



A lab could keep:

a manuscript starter structure for new papers
a workshop exercise template
a project website template with agreed navigation and style

Extensions become especially helpful when a project needs journal formats, icons, or specialized presentation behavior.






TipGood habit



Treat extensions like dependencies: document them clearly and only add them when they solve a real need.





For beginners, the key lesson is not memorizing many extensions. It is learning that Quarto can grow with them, from simple documents to more customized publishing workflows.

title: Exercise: Plan a Simple Dashboard
text:
Sketch a beginner-friendly Quarto dashboard concept for communicating research results to a non-specialist audience.


You should understand:

how Quarto organizes content into pages
the difference between static and interactive communication
the importance of choosing a clear audience




Your project needs a simple web-based summary for partners who want to monitor three indicators without reading a full report.




Identify the audience for the dashboard.
Choose three indicators or summaries that matter most to them.
Draft a page title and one-sentence purpose statement.
Plan a layout with an introduction, three metric areas, and a short interpretation section.
Write one note about what should stay simple in version one.
Add one sentence explaining where the underlying data would come from.




Use this sentence to begin:

This dashboard helps project partners monitor restoration progress across sites using a small set of regularly updated indicators.




Add an idea for one interactive feature, such as choosing a site or time period.



You should finish with a dashboard concept that:

has a defined audience
avoids trying to show everything at once
could later become a real Quarto dashboard project




Ask yourself:

Is the intended audience clear?
Are the selected indicators genuinely useful?
Does the plan stay focused enough for a first version?

title: Exercise: Draft a Manuscript Skeleton
text:
Create a starter Quarto manuscript structure that is ready for writing, review, and later expansion.


You should be comfortable with:

headings in Markdown
basic YAML metadata
the idea that Quarto can render to more than one format




You are preparing the first draft of a paper on land-use change and pollinator diversity. You want a manuscript skeleton that can later grow into a full article.




Create a new file called manuscript.qmd.
Add a YAML header with title, author, date, and outputs for HTML and DOCX.
Add headings for Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion, and References.
Under each heading, write one or two placeholder sentences.
Add a note where a figure or table will eventually appear.
If you already use citation files, add a placeholder bibliography entry in YAML.




---
title: "Pollinator Diversity Under Land-Use Change"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  html: default
  docx: default
bibliography: references.bib
---



Add a subsection under Methods for data collection and another for analysis.



You should end with a manuscript file that:

has a clear academic structure
is ready for collaborative expansion
can later support citations, figures, and tables




Ask yourself:

Does the document already look like the beginning of a paper?
Are the sections in a sensible order for your discipline?
Would a collaborator understand where to add new material?

title: Exercise: Build and Publish a Quarto Website
text:
By the end of this exercise you will have built a small Quarto website on your own computer and published it live on the internet — with a web address you can share. It is written for people who are completely new to Quarto, so each step explains what you are doing and why.






NoteNo coding required



A Quarto website is just a folder of plain-text pages. You can build and publish the whole thing without writing a single line of R or Python. If you later want to embed analysis, you can — but that is not needed here.




You will need three things:

Quarto installed, and an editor you are comfortable with. If you have not done this yet, follow Installing Quarto first. You can check your install by running quarto check in a terminal.
A terminal (the Terminal app, PowerShell, or the terminal panel inside your editor). Every command below is typed there.
A free GitHub account, which you will only need for the publishing step near the end. If you do not have one, you can sign up at github.com — or skip Step 6 and still complete everything else locally.




A small site with two pages — a home page and an about page — connected by a navigation bar at the top, given a colour theme, and finally published to the web. It is intentionally tiny: the point is to see the full path from empty folder to live website.



Quarto can generate a ready-to-use website folder for you. In your terminal, move to wherever you keep projects and run:
quarto create project website my-site
This makes a new folder called my-site containing a few starter files:

_quarto.yml — the project’s settings file. It controls things that apply to the whole site, like its title, the navigation bar, and the colour theme.
index.qmd — your home page. (index is the special name web servers look for first.)
about.qmd — a second example page.
styles.css — a place for custom styling (you will swap this for a nicer option in Step 5).







TipPrefer buttons to typing?



You do not have to use the terminal to create the project. The same wizard is available through your editor: in Positron or VS Code open the command palette and choose Quarto: Create Project → Website Project; in RStudio use New Project → Quarto Website. The result is the same starter folder.





Move into the new folder and start a live preview:
cd my-site
quarto preview
Your browser opens with the site already running. Leave this command going while you work — every time you save a file, the preview refreshes automatically so you can see changes instantly. Press Ctrl + C in the terminal to stop it later.



Open _quarto.yml. This is the one file that ties the site together. A minimal version looks like this:
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "My Research Site"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
Reading it from top to bottom:

project: type: website tells Quarto to treat the whole folder as one website rather than a single document.
website: title: is the name shown in the browser tab and in the top-left of the navigation bar. Change it to something of your own.
navbar: is the menu across the top. Each entry has an href (the page it links to) and the text shown for it. Adding a page to your site is mostly a matter of adding a line here.
format: html: sets options for every page at once — here, the cosmo theme and a table of contents (toc).

Change the title to your own and save. Watch the preview update.



Open index.qmd. Below the --- header, the content is just Markdown — the same headings, lists, links, and callouts you have already met in this workshop. Replace the starter text with a short welcome:
---
title: "Welcome"
---

## About this site

A small site I built while learning Quarto. Use the menu above to look around.
Now add a third page. Create a file called resources.qmd next to the others:
---
title: "Resources"
---

A few links and materials I want to share.
A new file is not visible to visitors until you link it. Add it to the navbar in _quarto.yml:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About
      - href: resources.qmd
        text: Resources
Save and check that the new menu item appears and works in the preview.



The quickest way to restyle a site is to pick a different built-in theme. Quarto ships with many; try swapping cosmo for another such as flatly, litera, or journal in _quarto.yml and watch the preview change.
To go a little further, you can override individual colours with a small style file. Create a file named styles.scss containing:
/*-- scss:defaults --*/

// a couple of colour tweaks
$navbar-bg: #2c3e50;
$link-color: #18bc9c;
The /*-- scss:defaults --*/ line at the top is required — it tells Quarto these are style variables. Then layer it on top of your theme in _quarto.yml:
format:
  html:
    theme: [cosmo, styles.scss]
    toc: true
(You can now remove the original styles.css line if your config still references it.)






NoteGoing further with branding



Picking colours by hand is fine for one site. When a whole research group or project wants a shared, consistent look across its website, slides, and documents, Quarto offers a dedicated branding file — see Using brand.yml in the advanced topics. Treat that as a later step; clear structure matters more than polish when you are starting out.





Your site only exists on your laptop so far. GitHub Pages is a free service that turns a GitHub repository into a live website, and Quarto can publish to it with a single command.
First, put your project on GitHub:

On github.com, create a new, empty repository (no README) named, for example, my-site.
Back in your terminal, inside the project folder, connect your files to it:

git init
git add .
git commit -m "First version of my site"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/my-site.git
git push -u origin main
Now publish:
quarto publish gh-pages
Quarto renders your site, copies the finished pages onto a special branch called gh-pages, and switches on GitHub Pages for you. After a minute or so it prints your live address, which will look like https://YOUR-USERNAME.github.io/my-site/. Open it — your site is on the internet.






TipWhat is the gh-pages branch?



It is a separate branch that holds only the built website (the HTML), kept apart from your editable source files on main. You normally never touch it by hand — quarto publish manages it for you.








NotePublishing automatically



Running a command each time works well, but you can also have the site rebuild itself every time you push a change. This very workshop site does that with a small GitHub Actions workflow file, so no one has to remember to publish. Once you are comfortable with the manual command, the Quarto publishing docs show how to set the automatic version up.





Edit one of your pages — add a sentence to the home page, for instance — and save. Then run:
quarto publish gh-pages
again. Refresh your live URL after a moment and your update is there. This edit-and-republish loop is the whole rhythm of maintaining a Quarto website.



If you finish early, try one of these:

Add a footer to the bottom of every page using a page-footer: entry under website:.
Move your navigation into a sidebar instead of (or as well as) the top navbar.
Add a fourth page and link it.
Try a custom Google Font by importing it in styles.scss and setting mainfont: in the HTML format options.




You should now have:

a small Quarto website project with at least two linked pages,
a colour theme of your choosing,
and a live URL you can share with anyone.




Ask yourself:

Does the site open and refresh when I run quarto preview?
Does every item in the navigation bar go to the right page?
Can I visit my site at its github.io address from a different browser or device?
Could I add another page and republish without looking up the steps?

title: Exercise: Build and Publish a Quarto Website
text:
You will need three things:

Quarto installed, and an editor you are comfortable with. If you have not done this yet, follow Installing Quarto first. You can check your install by running quarto check in a terminal.
A terminal (the Terminal app, PowerShell, or the terminal panel inside your editor). Every command below is typed there.
A free GitHub account, which you will only need for the publishing step near the end. If you do not have one, you can sign up at github.com — or skip Step 6 and still complete everything else locally.

title: Exercise: Build and Publish a Quarto Website
text: A small site with two pages — a home page and an about page — connected by a navigation bar at the top, given a colour theme, and finally published to the web. It is intentionally tiny: the point is to see the full path from empty folder to live website.

title: Exercise: Build and Publish a Quarto Website
text:
Quarto can generate a ready-to-use website folder for you. In your terminal, move to wherever you keep projects and run:
quarto create project website my-site
This makes a new folder called my-site containing a few starter files:

_quarto.yml — the project’s settings file. It controls things that apply to the whole site, like its title, the navigation bar, and the colour theme.
index.qmd — your home page. (index is the special name web servers look for first.)
about.qmd — a second example page.
styles.css — a place for custom styling (you will swap this for a nicer option in Step 5).







TipPrefer buttons to typing?



You do not have to use the terminal to create the project. The same wizard is available through your editor: in Positron or VS Code open the command palette and choose Quarto: Create Project → Website Project; in RStudio use New Project → Quarto Website. The result is the same starter folder.

title: Exercise: Build and Publish a Quarto Website
text:
Move into the new folder and start a live preview:
cd my-site
quarto preview
Your browser opens with the site already running. Leave this command going while you work — every time you save a file, the preview refreshes automatically so you can see changes instantly. Press Ctrl + C in the terminal to stop it later.

title: Markdown for Beginners
text:
Markdown is a lightweight writing syntax. Instead of clicking formatting buttons, you type small symbols directly into plain text. Quarto builds on standard Markdown and adds publishing features that are especially helpful for technical and academic work.


Markdown is worth learning because it is:

fast to write
easy to read in raw form
suitable for version control
flexible across websites, reports, and manuscripts

For workshop participants, Markdown is the main skill that unlocks most of Quarto.





# Main Title
## Section
### Subsection



*italic*
**bold**



- bullet item
- another bullet

1. first step
2. second step



[Quarto](https://quarto.org)
![Figure description](images/field-site.png)



Inline code looks like quarto render.
Code blocks look like this:
```bash
quarto render
```




Quarto keeps standard Markdown, but it also makes room for:

callout blocks for notes, warnings, and tips
cross-references for sections, figures, and tables
executable code chunks
layout features for richer publishing







TipBeginner mindset



You do not need to memorize every Markdown feature at once. For most workshop tasks, headings, lists, links, emphasis, and fenced code blocks are enough to get started.





A methods note written in Quarto might use:

headings to separate data collection, cleaning, and analysis
bullet points for decisions and open questions
links to protocols or repositories
fenced code blocks to show commands used in the workflow




Try writing a short research note with:

a title heading
one short paragraph
a list of three tasks
a link to a project repository or journal website




Continue to YAML Headers to see how Quarto stores document settings at the top of a file.

title: YAML Headers in Quarto
text:
YAML is the metadata block at the top of a Quarto document. It sits between two lines of --- and controls document information such as title, author, date, output formats, and execution settings.


YAML is where Quarto moves from “plain text note” to “configured publication”. A well-chosen header can make the same source file render as a report, manuscript, slide deck, or website page.



---
title: "Fieldwork Notes"
author: "A. Researcher"
date: today
format: html
---





This includes the title, subtitle, authors, date, and sometimes affiliations or keywords.



This tells Quarto what to create, such as HTML, PDF, DOCX, or revealjs slides.



This controls how code chunks behave across the file, for example whether code is shown or whether warnings are hidden.




A manuscript draft might use a header like this:
---
title: "How Soil Moisture Shapes Restoration Outcomes"
author:
  - "Research Team"
format:
  html: default
  docx: default
  pdf: default
bibliography: references.bib
---
That one header already signals a multi-format workflow.






WarningCommon beginner issue



YAML is sensitive to indentation. If spacing is inconsistent, rendering can fail even when the content looks almost correct.






YAML configures the document
Markdown writes the document
code chunks generate results inside the document

Keeping these roles separate makes Quarto much easier to understand.



Continue to Installing Quarto for a practical overview of what participants need on their machine.

title: One Tool to Rule Them All
text:
Start with the foundations →
Jump to use cases
Quarto is an open-source scientific and technical publishing system created by Posit. It helps researchers move from scattered workflows to a more integrated way of writing, documenting, presenting, and sharing their work.
This workshop website is designed as a guided reference. You can follow it from top to bottom during a live session, or return to individual pages later when you need help with a specific output format.

title: One Tool to Rule Them All
text:
Who We Are

title: One Tool to Rule Them All
text:
Who This Workshop Is For
This material is especially useful for:

Early career researchers building their first durable workflow
Experienced researchers who want fewer disconnected tools
Teams that care about reproducibility, transparency, and reuse
Anyone who wants websites, manuscripts, presentations, and notes to feel part of the same system

Basic familiarity with at least one programming language such as R, Python, or Julia can help, but it is not required for most of the lessons here.

title: One Tool to Rule Them All
text:
Why Quarto Matters For Researchers
In a nutshell, academic work usually produces and involves many related outputs:
We take rough notes and exploratory analysis in the early stages of research. These then evolve into polished text as well as figures and tables (which are often produced programmatically). We then combine these into manuscripts for publication, talks for conferences or sometimes reports and interactive communication for stakeholders or project partners. At the same time, there is an increasing need to share our work in other forms of public outreach and build our own online presence. Typically, many of these activities are done in using tools that are not necessarily integrated or compatible, which can lead to a fragmented workflow and a lot of time spent on manual formatting.
Quarto makes all of these activities easier to manage by allowing you to perform them in a single system. You can write your notes, code, and text in plain text files, and then render them into different formats (like HTML, PDF, DOCX, slides) without having to switch tools or worry about compatibility. This means you can focus more on the content of your research and less on the technicalities of formatting and tool management.






NoteWorkshop idea



The core promise of Quarto is simple: write once in a plain text source file and render to multiple professional outputs such as HTML, PDF, DOCX, and slides.

title: One Tool to Rule Them All
section: How To Use This Site & What You Will Learn
text:
How To Use This Site & What You Will Learn
We have broken down the workshop into six main sections (with an advanced section for those who want to go deeper). Each section has a separate page with a guided exercise to help you practice the workflow with an academic scenario. You don’t have to follow the sections in order, or indeed complete them all, but we recommend starting with the foundations if you are new to Quarto.



Foundations
Markdown, YAML, and Quarto’s project model: the building blocks every output shares.




Lab notebooks
Reproducible research, from rough exploration to a journal-ready PDF.




Manuscripts
Write in plain text; render to PDF, HTML, or DOCX, then share with collaborators.




Slides
Build reveal.js talks from the same source as your paper.




Websites
Project, lab, and personal sites with Quarto’s website mode.




Dashboards & brand
Interactive dashboards, plus brand.yml for one consistent visual identity.









WarningBefore you start



Make sure Quarto ≥ 1.5 is installed and that you can run quarto check in your terminal. If you hit issues, see the Installing Quarto page.

title: 1 Product
text:
brand
Split surface: the landing page and overall identity are brand (the design is part of the message), while the interior reference pages lean product (design serves fast lookup). PRODUCT.md carries brand as the default; override to product when working purely on interior docs utility.



Researchers, across two arrival paths:

In a live workshop session, following the site top-to-bottom while learning.
Returning later, jumping straight to one page when stuck on a specific output format.

Spanning early-career researchers building their first durable workflow, experienced researchers tired of juggling disconnected tools, and teams that care about reproducibility, transparency, and reuse. Mixed technical background: comfortable with at least one of R / Python / Julia is helpful but not assumed.
The job to be done: learn to author once in plain text and render to many professional outputs (HTML, PDF, DOCX, slides, dashboards, websites) without switching tools, then keep the site as a reference.



A guided workshop and standing reference for Quarto, the open-source scientific and technical publishing system. It moves researchers from scattered authoring workflows to a single integrated system, organised as Foundations → Academic Use Cases → Advanced Topics, each section paired with a hands-on exercise grounded in a real academic scenario.
Success: a researcher leaves able to produce a genuine output (lab notebook, manuscript, talk, or website) in Quarto, and comes back to the site when they need it again. The site is also a working demonstration: built in Quarto itself, it is evidence of what the tool can produce.



Scholarly and editorial. Warm, literate, quietly confident, the feel of a well-made academic publication rather than a software landing page. The voice is clear, specific, and generous; it explains the why, not only the how.

Three words: literate, credible, unhurried.
Emotional goal: a researcher should feel that adopting Quarto is a calm, sensible upgrade in capable hands, with no hype.





Generic default docs theme — the out-of-the-box Bootstrap/Quarto look: undifferentiated and clearly templated.
Corporate SaaS landing — gradient hero, hero-metric stat blocks, identical feature-card grids, buzzword copy.
Overdesigned / flashy — heavy animation, glassmorphism, decorative effects that compete with learning.
AI-slop card scaffolding — the current homepage subject cards and after-body nav cards lean on numbered section markers (01 · / 02 ·) and colored left-stripe borders, both recognised tells. Treat these as the first thing to rework, not a pattern to extend.





Practice what you preach. The site is itself exemplary Quarto output; every page is proof the tool can make beautiful, credible work. This is why the landing is brand even though interior reference pages serve their content like a product.
Teach by showing. Real rendered output and runnable code over abstract description.
Reference-first navigation. A learner mid-session and a returner-in-a-hurry both find the right page fast; structure and findability beat decoration on interior pages.
Earn every flourish. Warmth comes from typography, warm paper, and the quartile accents, not from effects. If a decorative element does not aid comprehension, cut it.
Credible, not corporate. Scholarly restraint throughout; no marketing cadence, no hype.




Target WCAG 2.1 AA.

Body text ≥ 4.5:1 against its background (watch muted text on warm paper); large text ≥ 3:1.
Visible keyboard focus and full keyboard navigability.
Full prefers-reduced-motion alternatives for any motion.
Both the light and dark brand.yml themes must pass contrast.
Content must stay usable when rendered to other Quarto formats (PDF / DOCX) and at small viewports on shared screens during a live session.

title: Documenting Research in Progress
text:
Quarto works well as a lab notebook or research log because it combines readable text, structured metadata, and optional code in one plain text document. That makes it a strong fit for exploratory work that changes over time.


Research in progress generates many small but important pieces of context:

what you tried
what worked and failed
which files or datasets you used
what to do next

A Quarto notebook helps capture that context in a durable and shareable format.





Markdown headings make it easy to separate entries by date, experiment, or task.



YAML can record authorship, date, project name, and output preferences.



If you are using R or Python, you can place data summaries or quick visual checks directly beside your notes.



An HTML version may be useful for browsing, while PDF can work for archiving or sharing.

text:
Research in progress generates many small but important pieces of context:

what you tried
what worked and failed
which files or datasets you used
what to do next

A Quarto notebook helps capture that context in a durable and shareable format.

title: Creating Presentations
text:
Quarto can create slide decks using formats such as RevealJS. This is useful for researchers who want presentations to live close to the same material as their reports, papers, and websites.


Talks often reuse the same ideas, figures, and summaries that also appear in manuscripts or project documentation. A shared authoring workflow helps reduce duplication and keeps messaging consistent.





In a Quarto slide deck, level-two headings usually create new slides.



Speaker notes, figures, and short summaries can be adapted from existing Quarto material.



HTML slides are easy to share and update, and they work well for teaching or conference talks.




A conference presentation can be produced from the same project that contains the paper draft and supporting notes. That makes it easier to keep claims, numbers, and figures aligned.






NotePresentation mindset



A good slide deck is not a full paper on screen. Quarto makes it easy to write too much, so concise content still matters.
